coredata.dc:title: Sulfur doped graphene quantum dots as a potential sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of quercetin
coredata.dc:creator: Kadian, Sachin
Manik, Gaurav
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel, selective and sensitive fluorescent probe (sulfur doped graphene quantum dots, SGQDs) was designed for real-time detection of quercetin in red wine samples. SGQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid (CA) and 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) and characterized through advanced techniques. It was observed that fluorescence intensity of SGQDs could be substantially quenched by the addition of quercetin through inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. Additionally, a visual color change (colorless to light yellow) was also noticed after addition of quercetin into a solution of SGQDs. The change in SGQDs fluorescence intensity with varying quercetin content revealed good linearity in the 0–50.0 μM range with regression coefficient of 0.9943 and a lowest detection limit of 0.006 μg/mL. To authenticate the real-time application of SGQDs as a potential fluorescent probe, red wine samples having different quercetin concentrations were used for quantitative analysis, after the optimization of several analytical parameters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur doped graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent probe
Quercetin
Inner filter effect
Quenching
Pharmaceutical assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of sulfur doped graphene quantum dots (SGQDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Human Resources and Development
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education and Human Resources Development

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The first author is grateful to Ministry of Human Resources and Development (MHRD), Government of India for the doctoral fellowship. The authors would like to express their gratitude towards Institute Instrumentation Centre and Center of Nanotechnology, IIT Roorkee for providing the facilities for advanced characterizations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Chemical structure of quercetin and (b) Schematic diagram of synthesis process of SGQDs and detection of quercetin.
(a) X-Ray Diffraction (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra (Inset: Image of SGQDs in the absence and presence of UV lamp) (c) PL spectra and (d) FTIR spectrum of as-synthesis SGQDs.

(a) Full scan XPS spectrum (b) High resolution C 1s spectrum (c) High resolution S 2p spectrum of as-synthesized SGQDs. The black and red lines display the raw data and fitted curve of black line, respectively. Other color lines are fitted curves for specific bonds. (d) Influence of reaction time on fluorescence intensity of SGQDs-Quercetin complex (e) Effect of pH on SGQDs and (f) Effect of ethanol (70%) on fluorescence intensity of SGQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) SGQDs fluorescence response to different concentration (0–50.0 μM) of quercetin (b) Stern-Volmer plot between F 0/F and concentration of quercetin (c) Real-time image of SGQDs-Quercetin complex with varying concentration (0–50 μM) of quercetin (d) Overlapping between UV–Vis absorption spectra of quercetin and SGQDs excitation and emission spectra (e) Lifetime decay of SGQDs and SGQDs-Quercetin complex and (f) Selectivity assay of fluorescent probe in the presence of other flavonoids.
Analytical results of quercetin in red wine samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: RSD: Relative Standard Deviation = (Standard Deviation / Average) × 100.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of various quercetin detection methods with proposed fluorescent probe.

entry.cross-ref: Park, Kim, Rehman, Na, and Yoo (2016)
Yola, Gupta, Eren, Şen, and Atar (2014)

head.ce:title: Sulfur doped graphene quantum dots as a potential sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of quercetin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer and Process Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Roorkee, Uttarakhand, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer and Process Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer and Process Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Roorkee, Uttarakhand, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel, selective and sensitive fluorescent probe (sulfur doped graphene quantum dots, SGQDs) was designed for real-time detection of quercetin in red wine samples. SGQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid (CA) and 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) and characterized through advanced techniques. It was observed that fluorescence intensity of SGQDs could be substantially quenched by the addition of quercetin through inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. Additionally, a visual color change (colorless to light yellow) was also noticed after addition of quercetin into a solution of SGQDs. The change in SGQDs fluorescence intensity with varying quercetin content revealed good linearity in the 0–50.0 μM range with regression coefficient of 0.9943 and a lowest detection limit of 0.006 μg/mL. To authenticate the real-time application of SGQDs as a potential fluorescent probe, red wine samples having different quercetin concentrations were used for quantitative analysis, after the optimization of several analytical parameters.

ce:section.ce:para: In last few years, flavonoids have attracted academia and scientific community due to their major role in preventing some cardiovascular diseases and hormonally dependent human cancer (Van Elswijk, Schobel, Lansky, Irth, & Van Der Greef, 2004). Quercetin (3ʹ,3,4ʹ,5,7-pentahydroxyflavone), is a most common natural bioflavonoid compound found in leguminous plants, fruits, vegetables, herbs, seeds, red wine and tea (Wang, Wang, & Zhu, 2003). The catechol ring and the hydroxyl groups present in chemical structure (Fig. 1 a) at positions 3′, 4′, 3, 5 and 7 makes quercetin a potential candidate to be used as a radical scavenger, anti-blood platelet, antiviral, anti-carcinogenic, expanding blood vessel and anti-inflammatory agent because of antioxidant properties of quercetin (Yang et al., 2012). Due to its pharmacological, biological and biochemical activities, detection or determination of quercetin for pharmaceutical chemistry, biochemistry and clinical medicine is very important. Recently, various methods including chromatographic, spectrophotometric, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and capillary electrophoresis methods (Berger, Wein, Blank, Metges, & Wolffram, 2012; Dar, Naikoo, Hassan, & Shaikh, 2016; Memon et al., 2017; Wang, Lin, Ma, Xu, & Lin, 2016) have been reported for quantitative detection of quercetin.

Despite substantial progress in the detection of quercetin, there remains enormous scope to develop an ultra-sensitive, selective, and low-cost novel strategy with ease of operation. In this regard, the fluorescence analytical methods may be suitable alternative with all mentioned desired characteristics for the detection of various organic and inorganic analytes. Fluorescence based detection of such analytical techniques is dependent on the luminescent behavior of metallic nanoclusters, quantum dots (QDs) and organic fluorescent dyes. QDs having diameter (2–10 nm) and exhibiting advanced optical properties as compared with traditional fluorophores have fascinated researchers interest globally. The unique properties such as, broad absorption range, good extinction coefficient, narrow line width in emission spectrum, high quantum yield and tunable emission maxima make QDs a possible fluorophore for in vitro and in vivo investigation (Medintz, Uyeda, Goldman, & Mattoussi, 2005). For instance, QDs have been recently used for detection of organic compounds as well as inorganic ions such as, auramine O sensing, curcumin, luteolin, Ag+, Fe3+, Pb+, Hg+ and Cu+ (Bian et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2015). However, research on the detection of quercetin using QDs as a fluorescent probe is still limited. In the past, sporadic research groups have discussed the application of fluorescent nanoprobe for quercetin sensing. For instance, Xiao et al. synthesized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) for the determination of quercetin (Xiao, Yuan, He, & Gao, 2013). Wu and Chen discussed the usage of ZnS QDs in quercetin detection (Wu & Chen, 2014). Later, Zou et al. introduced with organosilane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs), potential sensing probe for quercetin detection (Zou et al., 2015). Furthermore, usually, semiconductor QDs have been studied as fluorescence assay probe due to high photoluminescent quantum yield and chemical stability. However, these QDs are synthesized under rigorous experiment conditions and/or high-cost using toxic heavy metals as raw material. Therefore, it has become essential to introduce a novel fluorescent probe for quercetin detection that are economic and safer for humans as well as environment.

Recently, a new kind of fluorescent carbon derived graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted interest of scientific community in various fields including chemo-sensors, biosensor, bioimaging, drug delivery, optoelectronics and photocatalysis due to their low toxicity, high aqueous solubility, tunable photoluminescence (PL), and desirable biocompatibility. Moreover, in comparison to traditional semiconductor QDs, GQDs could be easily synthesized through cost-effective one step bottom-up approach by using low-cost raw precursors. Although GQDs possess most of these properties but, their relatively low fluorescence quantum yield leads to a compromise in fluorescence based analytical applications. Heteroatoms (B, N, S, F and P) doping into the framework of GQDs could enhance the fluorescence quantum yield by improving their optical and electronic properties (Kadian, Manik, Kalkal, Singh, & Chauhan, 2019; Ye et al., 2013). For example, Bian et al. synthesized B and N co‐doped carbon dots (BNCDs) through microwave heating for the curcumin detection (Bian et al., 2018). Among these dopants, N and S are primarily considered as attractive heteroatoms to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield of GQDs (Luo et al., 2018). Till date, sulfur doped GQDs (SGQDs) have not been studied as potential fluorescent probe for the detection of quercetin. In the present work, we have synthesized SGQDs through simple bottom-up approach by pyrolyzing citric acid (CA) and 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), as cost-effective raw precursors. The as-synthesized SGQDs were utilized as a fluorescent sensing probe for the detection of quercetin (Fig. 1b). The quenching of fluorescence intensity of SGQDs by the addition of quercetin through inner filter effect (IFE) has been examined. Further, the effect of pH, solvent, incubation time, concentration of quercetin and other co-existing flavonoid compounds on fluorescence intensity of SGQDs were discussed. Under the optimal parameters, the fluorescent probe was validated for quercetin detection and successfully practiced for red wine sample analysis.

CA and MPA were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from Merck India. Quercetin, luteolin, apigenin, hesperetin, genistein, daidzein, malvidin, myricetin and kaempferol were procured from SRL India. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS; pH 7.0) and 70% ethanol solution used in experiments were prepared fresh for each experiment. All the reagents used in experiments were of analytical grade and used as received. Fresh Millipore water was used for dilutions and preparation of aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sulfur doped graphene quantum dots (SGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: SGQDs were prepared using previously reported method with minor modifications through bottom-up approach by direct pyrolysis of CA and MPA (Dong et al., 2012; Kadian et al., 2019). In this process, 2.1 g CA and 300 μL MPA were mixed in a 30 mL beaker and allowed to heat up at 200 °C using hot plate for 10 min. The obtained orange color liquid was mixed into freshly prepared NaOH solution (50 mL, 10 mg/mL) and left under vigorous stirring for 30 min. The pH of resultant mixture was maintained at 7.0 and filtered using syringe filter having pore size 0.22 μm. Further, the filtered solution was subjected to dialysis using 3 kDa dialysis bag for purification. The final product was kept at 4 °C until further use and characterization.

The optical properties fluorescence spectra of as-synthesized SGQDs were collected with F-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer ranging from 300 to 600 nm wavelength and UV–visible spectra was recorded using Cary 5000 UV–Vis-NIR spectrometer within the 200 to 800 nm wavelength range. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was conducted using Perkin Elmer FT-IR C91158, UK, in the range of wavenumber 4000 to 500 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8-Advance) at an accelerating potential of 40 kV with Cu Kα radiation source. In addition, the elemental analysis of detection probe was observed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Physical Electronics, PHI 5000 Versa Probe III).

Both PBS (1.0 mL) and aqueous solution of as-synthesized SGQDs (0.05 mL) were mixed in a 3 mL glass cuvette followed by addition of quercetin solution (0.1 mL, 200 μM) and mixed thoroughly for 10 min. Final mixture was transferred into quartz cuvette for detection of quercetin. For quantitative analysis of quercetin, fluorescence spectra were recorded using the parameters as: excitation wavelength (λ Ex) = 360 nm, emission wavelength (λ Em) = 460 nm, voltage = 700 V, temperature = 25 °C and slit widths = 5 nm, 5 nm. All the experiments and measurements were conducted in triplicate under same experimental condition.

The most common beverage (i.e. red wine, Shiraz-Cabernet Sauvignon, Australia) having high content of quercetin was purchased from local supermarket for real sample analysis of proposed fluorescent probe. The red wine samples having quercetin concentration (15 μg/mL) were filtered using 0.45 μm membrane filter to remove any undesired impurities and stored at 4 °C before analysis. A stock solution of standard quercetin with known concentration (200 μM) was freshly prepared in 70% ethanol for recovery assay. All the experiments were performed under same circumstances with n = 5.

As reported earlier, moderate pyrolysis process can partially carbonize CA to prepare GQDs having small sp2 cluster isolated inside the graphitic structure (Dong et al., 2012). In order to tailor the optical, electronic, surface state and local chemical structure properties, MPA was introduced to selectively dope GQDs with sulfur atom using previously reported bottom-up approach with minor modifications (Kadian et al., 2019). These freshly prepared SGQDs were characterized for confirmation before their use as fluorescent assay probe for quercetin detection. Fig. 2 a shows XRD diffraction pattern with broad peak (0 0 2) centered at around 24°, consistent with previous reports and which preliminary confirms that a grapheme-like structure has formed (L. Li et al., 2014). Next, the optical properties of SGQDs were recorded using UV-Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectroscopy. The characteristic absorption peak at 360 nm shown in Fig. 2b represent n-π* electronic transitions due to existing C-OH or CO in the structure of SGQDs (Dong et al., 2012; Luo et al., 2018). The absorption band positioned at 360 nm may correspond to optimal PL excitation peak of SGQDs. The pale-yellow color aqueous solution of SGQDs emitted blue color (Fig. 2b, inset) fluorescence under UV radiation of wavelength 365 nm, illustrating its excellent luminescence and dispersion properties. Fig. 2c depicts the PL emission spectra of SGQDs at different excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 400 nm, signifying excitation-independent emission. This excitation-independent emission behavior, suggested the uniformity of either the size of synthesized SGQDs or emissive sites present in formed sp2 cluster (Li et al., 2013). This kind of emission behavior might also be helpful in condensing the interfering effect of autofluorescence during detection of analyte (Luo et al., 2018). It could be observed that emission was maximum at wavelength (λEm) of 460 nm for an optimal excitation wavelength (λEx) of 360 nm, which is in good agreement with UV–Vis absorption spectra results. The observed ~100 nm Stokes’ shift among excitation and emission spectra of SGQDs demonstrates a great potential of SGQDs for analytical application.

FTIR spectra was performed to examine the presence of relevant functional groups and chemical structure characteristic of SGQDs. The FTIR spectrum of SGQDs in Fig. 2d revealed a broad band centered at 3325 cm−1, assigned to stretching vibration corresponding to O–H group. The peak at 3325 cm−1 is a sign of hydrophilic nature of SGQDs (Qu et al., 2013). A shoulder peak at 1730 cm−1 and band at 1635 cm−1 shows the presence of carboxylic group and bending vibration of CC, respectively. Two more peaks at 1229 and 1370 cm−1 are ascribed to symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of SO. An additional characteristic band present at 630 cm−1 associated with C–S stretching (Qu et al., 2013; Vinayan et al., 2016), indicates the presence of sulfur atom in the framework of GQDs. Meanwhile, the valence state and elemental composition of SGQDs were determined by XPS. The full scan XPS spectrum of SGQDs demonstrates the existence of C, O and S atoms. As shown in Fig. 3 a, SGQDs contains four predominant peaks at binding energy 284, 531, 228 and 163 eV corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, S 2s and S 2p, respectively. The peaks observed at binding energy 228 and 163 eV confirmed that S atom was successfully doped into the framework of GQDs (F. Li et al., 2018). The high-resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. 3b) contained three different peaks ascribed to CC, C–S and CO/C–O at binding energy 284.8, 285.73 and 289.21 eV, respectively (Kadian et al., 2019). As sulfur is less electronegative than oxygen, the C–S peak is located at a lower binding energy compared to C–O peak (Gao et al., 2017). The S 2p spectra shown in Fig. 3c consists of two peaks at 164.30 and 165.47 eV deconvoluted from single peak originally positioned at 163 eV. The former peak at 164.30 eV is ascribed to 2p 1/2 and 2p 3/2 sites of the –C–S–C– covalent bond whereas the latter peak at 165.47 eV corresponds to –C–SOx– bond present in SGQDs structure (Luo et al., 2018). The existence of appropriate peaks corresponding to carboxylic group, hydroxyl group, sulfide, sulfate and sulfones in XPS is in good accordance with FTIR spectra, thereby, confirming the successful synthesis of SGQDs.

It is known that the fluorescence intensity of analytical probe can be influenced by various parameters including incubation or reaction time, acidity (pH) and solvent (Kadian et al., 2018). Therefore, several tests were performed to optimize the parameters that influence effective detection of quercetin. First, to determine the kinetic response of detection probe, the influence of incubation time on fluorescence intensity of SGQDs after addition of 50 μM quercetin was investigated. Fig. 3d displays that SGQDs-quercetin (50 μM) complex can completely react in 10 min with an effective reduction in fluorescence intensity, suggesting that incubation time for sensing study should be 10 min. Hence, all the analytical experiments were performed after 10 min incubation time.

Next, the influence of pH (2.0–11.0) on fluorescence intensity of SGQDs was studied and shown in Fig. 3e. It can be observed that the fluorescence intensity of SGQDs increased quickly in the pH range of 2.0–7.0 and saturated thereafter to a constant value with no significant change in the pH range of 7.0–11.0. The increase in fluorescence intensity of SGQDs in pH range of 2.0–7.0 might be possible due to protonation process of carboxylate and sulfinyl groups present on surface of SGQDs.

The unchanged fluorescence emission of SGQDs seen (Fig. 3e) in the pH range from 7.0 to 11.0 probably occurred due to deprotonation equilibrium process explained earlier (Luo et al., 2018). Within the stable pH range (7.0–11.0), the fluorescence intensity of SGQDs was maximized at 9.0 (Fig. 3e), indicating that a pH of 9.0 was optimal for detection of quercetin.

Furthermore, ethanol (70%) was used as solvent for the preparation of different quercetin samples. For effective detection of analyte, it is important to investigate the effect of solvent volume on fluorescence intensity of sensing probe. Fig. 3f demonstrates that no significant change was observed in the fluorescence intensity of SGQDs even after increasing the volume of solvent up to 250 μL, indicating that ethanol did not inhibit the fluorescence intensity of sensing probe and may be used for subsequent experiments.

After exploring the optimal condition, SGQDs were used as a fluorescent probe for detection of quercetin. As shown in PL spectra (Fig. 2c), the fluorescence emission was maximum at 460 nm emission wavelength (λ Em) with an excitation wavelength (λ Ex) of 360 nm. This motivated the investigation of fluorescent probe sensing capabilities of SGQDs at different quercetin concentrations ranging from 0 to 50.0 μM, using λ Ex = 360 nm and λ Em = 460 nm as excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively. Fig. 4 a shows that fluorescence emission of SGQDs decreased significantly with increasing concentration of quercetin. Fig. 4b demonstrates the Stern-Volmer plot between Fo /F and concentration of quercetin, where Fo and F are initial and quenched fluorescence intensity, respectively. A good linear relationship between the quenched fluorescence intensity and quercetin concentration was observed in the range of 0 to 50.0 μM with a regression coefficient (R 2) of 0.99428. The limit of detection (LOD) for quercetin was calculated using the equation LOD = 3σ/k (where k was the slope of the linear calibration curve and σ was the standard error for n = 10 replicate determinations of the blank SGQDs solution) and estimated to be 0.006 μg/mL. Moreover, the sensitivity of fluorescent probe determined using slope of linear curve (Fig. 4b) was found to be 2.009 × 104 M−1, which is enough for quercetin sensing in real samples. Fig. 4c demonstrates the real-time image of SGQDs-Quercetin complex with varying concentration (0–50.0 μM) of quercetin. The apparent change in color or increasing opacity of SGQDs-Quercetin complex visually confirms the presence of quenching process. Additionally, this increasing opacity of SGQDs-Quercetin complex was further quantified and confirmed through UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. It can be seen from Fig. S1 (in Supplementary File) that the typical absorbance peak of SGQDs (360 nm) increased significantly with increasing concentration (0–50.0 μM) of quercetin, which is consistent with PL spectra results. Besides, Fig. S2 shows a good linear relationship between the absorbance intensity and concentration of quercetin with an R 2 value of 0.9884 which also conforms with PL spectra results. It can also be observed from Fig. S1 that a new absorption peak obtained at 315 nm, upstretched with increasing concentration (0–50.0 μM) of quercetin, may be ascribed to spectral features contributed by free quercetin in the solution. Hence, the aforementioned results enable us to conclude that SGQDs can be used as an environment friendly, simple and sensitive fluorescent probe for detection of quercetin.

The phenomenon of sensing probe fluorescence intensity reduction by numerous factors including molecular rearrangements, energy transfer, formation of ground state complexes, molecular interactions, excited-state reactions and collisional quenching, is known as fluorescence quenching (Xu, Chen, & Ma, 2018). Some common quenching mechanisms such as the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism and IFE mechanism have frequently been used in recent literature to detect multifarious analytes. The PET mechanism is generally accompanied by a reasonable change in the lifetime of fluorophore and has been used previously to determine hydroquinone (Ni et al., 2015). Next, the FRET mechanism occurs only when the emission spectrum of fluorophore overlaps with the absorption band of the quencher, has widely been used for biological aptasensor (Qian et al., 2014). The IFE mechanism ensues if the UV absorbance spectra of the quencher overlays complementarily with the PL excitation or emission spectra of fluorescent probe. This mechanism has also been widely used to sense chromium (VI), metronidazole, luteolin, picric acid, doxycycline, I− and H2O2 (Kadian, 2020; Sun et al., 2018). To understand the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of SGQDs-Quercetin complex, PL spectra, UV–Vis absorption spectra and lifetimes of SGQDs in absence and presence of quercetin were studied as shown in Fig. 4d. From Fig. 4d, it could be observed that the absorption spectra of quercetin did not overlap with emission spectra of SGQDs, eliminates the possibility of occurrence of FRET mechanism in the quenching process. Similarly, fluorescence lifetimes of SGQDs and SGQDs-Quercetin complex were studied (Fig. 4e) and found almost unchanged lifetime, illustrating that quenching may not be caused by PET mechanism. Further, it can be seen from Fig. 4d that the absorption spectrum of quercetin has a significant spectral overlap with the PL excitation spectra of SGQDs, indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of SGQDs-Quercetin complex might be possible due to presence of IFE mechanism (Sun et al., 2018). Moreover, the interaction mechanism of quencher and fluorescent probe can be classified into static and dynamic quenching. Static quenching is usually caused by the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex between the quencher and fluorescent molecule, whereas dynamic quenching occurs due to collision between fluorophore and quencher molecule (Xu et al., 2018). In order to confirm the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex between fluorophore and quencher “Hard and Soft Acids and Bases theory” (HSAB) was used (Wu & Chen, 2014). According to HSAB theory, phenolic hydroxyl groups present in chemical structure of quercetin belong to soft acids category and presence of relatively high polarizable donor atom (i.e. S atom) on the surface of SGQDs belong to soft bases category (Pearson, 1963). Consequently, strong acids of quercetin might have a strong interaction with soft bases of SGQDs and form conjugation of SGQDs-Quercetin complex. This complex might prevent the non-radiative electron to hole transfer and promote the release of decreasing recombinant fluorescence, resulting in decrement of concentration of fluorescent molecule. Furthermore, the static quenching process of interaction may be understood in terms of Stern-Volmer plot as shown in Fig. 4b. It has been reported that if the Stern-Volmer plot shows good linearity the quenching might be static and if it indicates a non-linear upward curvature it could be due to both dynamic and static quenching (Fraiji, Hayes, & Werner, 2009). In present case, the Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 4b) demonstrates good linearity which confirms the presence of static quenching and agrees with the explanation of HSAB theory. As a result, it may be concluded that the fluorescence quenching process of SGQDs was static in nature and possible due to presence of IFE mechanism.

To validate the selectivity of proposed sensing probe, fluorescence intensity of other flavonoids having similar chemical structure analogue including luteolin, apigenin, hesperetin, genistein, daidzein, malvidin, myricetin and kaempferol were measured and the histogram of fluorescence intensity response shown in Fig. 4f. Selectivity study indicates that almost no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of SGQDs was observed with the presence of other flavonoids (10 μM), while a sharp decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed due to the presence of same amount of quercetin (10 μM). This change in intensity may occur due to existence of IFE mechanism in the case of quercetin while no such mechanism prevailed for other co-existing flavonoid compounds due to difference of their standard UV–Vis absorption spectra. In summary, the developed fluorescent sensing probe displayed acceptable selectivity for quercetin and might be proficient to detect quercetin in the presence of interference substances.

To validate the real-time application of proposed fluorescent probe, three red wine samples with different quercetin concentration (1.5, 3.0 and 4.5 μg/mL) were used for quantitative analysis of quercetin. After the addition of 10 μg/mL standard solution of quercetin in each sample, recovery results of sensing probe were recorded through PL and UV–Vis spectroscopy and are listed with relative standard deviation (RSD) in Table 1 . The high degree of conformity between the results (Table 1) of both methods indicates that the proposed fluorescent probe possesses good accuracy and high selectivity towards quercetin in real sample analysis. A comparison between analytical performance of proposed fluorescent probe with traditional methods including HPLC, electrochemical and fluorescence for the detection of quercetin was also made and the major findings are shown in Table 2 . It can be observed that limit of detection obtained with our method is lower than other reports. Moreover, the recovery (94.2–96.1%) and RSD (1.1–2.2%) results of our proposed method are in acceptable range. Therefore, it can be concluded that our proposed SGQDs based fluorescent probe offers significant value for quercetin detection in real sample with good accuracy and high precision.

In summary, SGQDs were successfully synthesized and demonstrated to be a novel, rapid and sensitive fluorescent probe for quercetin detection. The fluorescence intensity of SGQDs was efficiently quenched in the presence of quercetin due to the complementary overlaps of absorption band of quercetin and excitation spectra of SGQDs due to the reported inner filter effect mechanism. The PL emission spectra of as-synthesized SGQDs showed excitation-independent emission, helpful in condensing the interfering effect of autofluorescence during detection. Under the optimal condition, the fluorescent probe possesses lowest limit of detection (0.006 µg/mL) with a good linear relationship between quenched fluorescence intensity and concentration of quercetin in a range of 0 to 50 μM. Moreover, the regression coefficient (0.99428) and selectivity (2.009 × 104 M−1) were also determined using slope of linear curve and found good enough to ensure quercetin is detected in real samples with ease. The interference study of proposed fluorescent probe performed in the presence of other flavonoids having similar chemical structure analogue also showed its strong selectivity for quercetin detection. Finally, the proposed detection strategy exhibited high precision, good accuracy and sensitivity when used for quantitative analysis of quercetin in red wine samples, with acceptable recovery (94.2–96.1%) and RSD (1.1–2.2%) values. As a result, the proposed method is expected to offer a considerable implication to detect other antibodies, biomarkers, or aptamers by partially modifying the sensing molecule and for further exploration in clinical and pharmaceutical assay of quercetin for real-world application.

Sachin Kadian: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Gaurav Manik: Conceptualization, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Writing - review & editing, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The first author is grateful to Ministry of Human Resources and Development (MHRD), Government of India for the doctoral fellowship. The authors would like to express their gratitude towards Institute Instrumentation Centre and Center of Nanotechnology, IIT Roorkee for providing the facilities for advanced characterizations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.126457.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
